paper_metadata.title: Do Thinking Tokens Help with Safety?

claims.claim: Refusal/compliance outcome is strongly predictable from the first token's hidden representation before any visible thinking begins
evidence: Trained logistic probe on first-token hidden representations achieves 0.84-0.95 AUROC across 4 model families
~88% balanced accuracy for predicting refusal/compliance from first token alone
5-fold stratified cross-validation with Youden's J cutoff selection

claims.claim: The thinking process resembles prefix completion more than deliberative revision
evidence: Final outcome rarely changes after approximately the first 20% of thinking
Continuation variance is very low given a fixed prefix
Qwen3-8B: 93% unchanged, Olmo-3-7B-Think: 91%, Phi-4-Reasoning: 70%, GPT-OSS-20B: 90%

claims.claim: ~74% of text-level deliberations occur when the response distribution is already locked to one refusal/compliance side
evidence: Three-auditor stance annotation of thinking traces (GPT-5.4, Gemini 3 Pro, Sonnet 4.6)
Fleiss κ=0.67 inter-annotator agreement
Majority-vote labels show ~74% of deliberation segments occur in 'locked' state

claims.claim: The Fisher discriminant shows a 'valley signature': high pre-decoding separability, sustained drop through middle, re-crystallization at end
evidence: Fisher discriminant ratio plotted across normalized thinking positions
Valley pattern reproduced across all 4 primary models and 5 supplementary models
Includes Qwen3-32B, Phi-4-Reasoning-Plus, Qwen3.5-9B, DeepSeek-R1 distills

claims.claim: Stance oscillations in thinking traces are predominantly performative rather than meaningful
evidence: Per-rollout and per-segment oscillation analysis
Most oscillation segments are 'Locked' (response distribution already fixed)
Only a small fraction are both 'Not Locked' and 'Significant' (meaningful)
Pattern holds across both harmful and benign prompt splits

claims.claim: Existing inference-time and training-based safety interventions shift behavior along the ASR-ORR tradeoff rather than improving it
evidence: Pareto frontier analysis of 9 defense methods × 4 base models
No defended cell improves on its base model in both ASR and ORR simultaneously
Every cell that lowers ASR does so by raising ORR; some are worse on both axes

claims.claim: Safety interventions suppress already-scarce deliberation signals
evidence: First-token readability remains high or increases after defense application
Oscillation counts decrease after defense (fewer deliberation segments)
Defenses push behavior toward over-refusal

claims.claim: Forcing extended thinking past the natural stopping point does not meaningfully change safety behavior
evidence: Budget-controlled experiments with early truncation and extended generation
Increasing thinking budget tends to raise compliance (higher ASR and lower ORR)
Forcing thinking past natural stopping yields little additional change

claims.claim: More permissive 'no-think to think' change measures also confirm limited impact of thinking on safety
evidence: Sign-only directional change analysis: most shifts are small
Mean signed change analysis: positive ASR improvements often come with negative ORR shifts
Phi-4-Reasoning shows a mild exception but overall conclusion holds

claim_relationships.C1_implies_C2: If the outcome is predictable from the first token, subsequent thinking is likely prefix completion
claim_relationships.C2_and_C3_imply_C5: If thinking is prefix completion and most deliberation happens when already locked, oscillations are performative
claim_relationships.C6_and_C7_imply_need: Current defenses fail to induce genuine deliberation, highlighting need for new methods
